dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to unit 7 for an unconscious diabetic patient at 1st floor apartment in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
My wife was found unconscious in her bed. We think it's low blood sugar from her diabetes.
Ma'am, are you responding to us now? Can you open your eyes for me?
She hasn't responded yet. She seems to have her eyes closed. Let me check for eye opening. Did she respond to your voice, Mr. Smith?
She hasn't moved or spoken. I'm not sure what's going on. Are you Mr. Smith? My name's Jason, we're paramedics. What's her name, please?
Okay, we're going to gently stimulate her arms and legs; see if she responds. Hold on, Mr. Smith, is she allergic to anything we need to know about right now?
Her airway is patent, but she has snoring respirations likely due to her altered mental status. Partner, let's check SpO2 now.
SpO2 is currently at 98%, which is normal. I'll also place the cardiac monitor to check for any arrhythmias.
Her breathing is at 18 per minute, clear. No signs of distress-she's not in respiratory difficulty right now. Partner, get her blood pressure.
Blood pressure is 129 over 74. Stable for now. She's lying comfortably where she was found. We're keeping her still and keeping an eye out for any changes.
Her skin is diaphoretic and clammy, which is consistent with hypoglycemic symptoms. She has strong, equal peripheral pulses. Are there any signs of trauma or injury, Mr. Smith?
Pulse is 66 and strong. We've previously documented that. Any next steps on your mind, Alex?
Her glucose is reading "low." Given protocol 3-8, I recommend administering intravenous Dextrose 10% to bring up her sugar levels.
Mr. Smith, what symptoms did she have before she passed out? Any chest pain, trouble breathing, or others?
She has Type 1 diabetes and was released from the hospital only yesterday. Today, she didn't eat much but took her insulin with dinner as usual.
She is 59 years old, weighs 100 kg, and has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes. She reported no injuries or trauma upon arrival.
She was unresponsive earlier but now she's moving her arms and legs. She does not report any pain. Mr. Smith, is there any reason you think she might be in pain she's not telling us about?
The EKG readout is normal sinus rhythm, so her heart activity is regular and stable. Ok to proceed with the Dextrose per protocol.
Her skin is now warming and drying. No signs of rashes, bites, or discoloration. I see no signs of trauma or that she's in pain right now.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. I see no signs of trauma to the head or neck. She's alerting with equal eye movement. She is alert, oriented (aox4), and aware of time and place now.
Her breath sounds are clear. No issues noted in the lungs or heart. She's not in respiratory distress, as observed and documented.
All extremities move full range without resistance. Her back is anatomically normal, with no signs of instability, pain or infection.
Dextrose 10% is ready. Let's proceed with 100 mL intravenous bolus. Partner, the IV is in place on her right arm. I'll start once I confirm placement.
IV started, 20G in right AC. Dextrose 10% administered. We'll continue to monitor her for response as outlined in protocol 3-8.
I've also placed a 4-lead ECG monitor. We are following protocol 3-8 for continuous cardiac monitoring.

Her glucose is up to 169 now, which is well in the normal range. She responded well to Dextrose. Skin return to normal and resolution of diaphoresis noted now, along with full alertness (aox4) per recovery.
Her GCS is at 15. Pupils at 4 mm and reactive. All her vitals read normal. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or nausea, post treatment.
She is alert, oriented, and shows no discomfort. She reports no pain and moves comfortably. She responded well to our intervention and is now active.
We followed protocol 3-8 for diabetic hypoglycemia. Glucose improved with Dextrose and her condition is stable. She has not opted for transport.
Ma'am, are you feeling better and capable of staying put with your family in the apartment, or would you prefer to be taken in for further observation at the hospital?
I feel completely fine now. I understand the risks, but I do not want to be transported right now.
Understood. Here's the transport refusal form. By signing, your decision is in writing. I'll read it over now. Ready?
Yes. I understand what the form says. My family and I made this decision together. I believe it is the best option now.
Refusal has been witnessed and signed by the patient and family. We are officially off the scene with no further obligations.
For our records, the patient refuses transport and is staying at her residence with family. I'm noting that now.
Please make sure to monitor for any worsening symptoms-like confusion, chest pain, or new symptoms-and call 911 if anything happens.
All family is present and understands the risks. Documents are printed and signed as per protocol. Paperwork is in order, Ma'am. Thank you for cooperating with us today.
We've checked D-C-A-P-B-T-L-S and found no abnormalities. Everything we checked is normal. Please contact us immediately if there is any change.
